title: Computer Systems Design Services

narrative.executive_view: Computer Systems Design Services is a conditional MSP/systems-integration roll-up screen, not a blanket software-services thesis. AI is already changing the routine operating layer: Kaseya's 2026 survey reports 53% of MSPs use AI to automate ticketing, patching and monitoring [S1]. The investable question is whether the operator retains that productivity while clients continue paying a recurring fee for accountable IT, security, documentation and incident response. Current evidence supports durable need and active consolidation, but it also shows smaller contracts and competitive pressure. The selected score is therefore conditional, with resale-heavy, project-led and founder-dependent firms outside the thesis.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The most exposed work is repeatable technical delivery: ticket triage, knowledge retrieval, password and onboarding flows, patching, monitoring, documentation, runbook drafting, configuration and first-pass QA. Kaseya reports current automation of ticketing, patching and monitoring, though more than half of respondents have automated only roughly a quarter of workload [S1]. Systems analysis, solution architecture, migrations, security escalation, field installs and client-specific environment judgment remain partial rather than complete substitutes. The role estimates reflect this task split, not deal activity or subscription economics.
narrative.value_capture: Capture is materially better than in time-and-materials consulting because managed services commonly bill per user, per device, bundled monthly fee or value-based subscription [S2]. That can let a provider keep labor savings until a renewal reprices, particularly for security and business-continuity work; Kaseya describes cybersecurity and backup as the revenue anchors in its current MSP survey [S1]. The counter-channel is explicit: per-user fees fall when a client reduces headcount, per-device fees can fall with a smaller estate, and vendors may monetize the automation layer. I therefore assign only partial distribution capture despite recurring billing and client documentation.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective operational adoption has crossed the 50% threshold: Kaseya's April 2026 survey of more than 1,000 MSPs reports that 53% are already using AI to automate ticketing, patching and monitoring [S1]. I set current adoption to 53% and t50 to zero under the frozen rule. This is strong business-model evidence but not a U.S. 541512 establishment census; the source's global MSP population also spans systems integrators, outsourced operations and related IT-service codes. It measures deployment in three core operational activities, not autonomous management of an entire client estate.
narrative.consolidation_economics: Buyability is real but quality-dependent. The immutable target count is large, and a specialist MSP exit adviser describes many firms as small, founder-led businesses launched in the 1990s and 2000s whose owners are now considering transitions [S5]. Solganick's July 2025 market update records a Q2 rebound, active private-equity interest and several MSP platform acquisitions, including a security-focused MSP joining Evergreen's Lyra family [S3]. I count only three demonstrably active platform/strategic buyers from that compact evidence and do not confuse their interest with proof that every low-margin local MSP is saleable.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE, though the delivery mix changes. SMBs still need someone accountable for endpoint security, backup, recovery, vendor coordination, privileged access, incident escalation and the business consequences of an outage. Kaseya reports security and business-continuity as the strongest growing MSP revenue categories [S1]. AI makes routine monitoring and service-desk effort cheaper; it does not presently establish that clients can safely replace a managed operator with a generic AI subscription. The key downside remains platform disintermediation or AI-driven client seat reductions, which would pressure fees even if the need for accountable operations persists.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main modelling limitation is frame mismatch. The frozen 541512 data includes systems design, while MSP surveys and deal sources also cover 541513, 541519, custom-development, MSSP and cloud-partner populations. Additionally, blended MSP revenue may include low-margin hardware and software resale, whereas valuation evidence rewards recurring revenue, retention and security/cloud depth [S2][S4]. Owner age and consolidation counts are directional rather than code-specific. The selected buy/exit pair is a modest scale-path assumption within the observed small-MSP valuation range, not a promise that a resale-heavy or weak-contract operator can exit at a platform multiple.

sources.title: Kaseya, 2026 State of the MSP Report press release
sources.what_it_supports: Survey of more than 1,000 MSPs worldwide: 53% already use AI for ticketing, patching and monitoring; 48% rank AI/automation as the leading client need; cybersecurity and business-continuity revenue are growing. Used for operational adoption and terminal-demand context.

sources.title: Kaseya, MSP Pricing: A Guide to Managed IT Services Pricing
sources.what_it_supports: MSP pricing modes include per-user, per-device, combined, tiered and fixed-fee subscriptions; the source also explains that per-user revenue fluctuates with client employee counts. Its benchmark figures are from the 2023 survey, so pricing evidence is older than adoption evidence.

sources.title: Solganick & Co., Technology Services M&A Market Update, 1H 2025
sources.what_it_supports: MSP M&A activity rebounded in Q2 2025; private-equity interest remained robust for predictable/scalable revenue, and the report lists current platform acquisitions including an Evergreen Lyra MSP deal. Its advisory focus is firms with roughly $2M–$20M EBITDA, broader than every target in the screen.

sources.title: Aventis Advisors, MSP Valuation Multiples
sources.what_it_supports: Advisor analysis says smaller MSPs typically trade around 5–8x EBITDA, while larger MSPs receive higher multiples; its disclosed-transaction median is 8.9x but the median deal size is $38.5M. Used for conservative buy/exit assumptions and size-discount caveats.

sources.title: Excendio Advisors, Navigating Growth, Exit & Legacy as an MSP Owner
sources.what_it_supports: MSP-focused exit adviser describes a large cohort of entrepreneurial founders from the 1990s and early 2000s, many now in their 50s or older, and discusses consolidation and transition challenges. It is advisory commentary, not a representative owner-age census.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Task exposure is high across coding, support, QA, documentation, monitoring and administrative delivery. It remains partial for architecture, security accountability, field work and client-specific judgment. The 4-digit role mix has a large residual likely containing both additional technicians and physical/field roles, so it is not assumed fully automatable.
caveats: Role mix is 4-digit 5415, while MSP and systems-integration employment spans adjacent NAICS codes.
Kaseya measures automation deployment, not labor hours eliminated by SOC role.
The residual includes unknown proportions of desk, field, infrastructure and administrative work.

role_judgments.rationale: Scripting, configuration, integration glue, documentation and routine remediation are highly exposed; architecture, difficult migrations and client-environment judgment remain.
Tier-1 ticketing, resets, triage and routine end-user requests are core targets of AI-enabled ticketing and monitoring; desk-side visits remain.
Reporting, capacity summaries and coordination automate, while vCIO judgment, incident ownership and senior technical decisions remain human.
Assessment drafts, inventory analysis, migration planning and gap analysis compress, while requirements elicitation and vendor-risk judgment persist.
Quoting, proposal drafting and renewal paperwork automate; local relationship selling and commercial negotiation remain.
Back-office operations and internal reporting are exposed, but owner-led customer relationships and organizational accountability are not fully replaceable.
General computer-operations work includes automation-friendly configuration, monitoring and runbook tasks, with some physical or specialist exception work retained.
Project status, documentation, scheduling and risk logs automate substantially; cross-party migration execution and escalation remain.
Data gathering, first-pass recommendations and report production compress; accountable change management and executive communication persist.
Test generation, regression analysis and defect triage are highly assistable, while acceptance responsibility and complex test design remain.

The residual likely contains additional helpdesk, dispatch, administration, technicians and field/infrastructure staff. It receives partial exposure to avoid treating physical work as ticket automation.

pi_dist.rationale: Per-user, per-device and fixed-fee subscriptions allow the MSP to capture some lower cost-to-serve before renewal, but seat-count contraction, competitive pricing and vendor licensing constrain retention.
caveats: The 2023 pricing survey predates the current service-desk AI cycle; client headcount and endpoint-base effects could offset rate retention.

pi_moat.rationale: Recurring operations, environment documentation, security/backup delivery and client trust create material switching friction; current deal evidence favors predictable scalable revenue. The moat is weaker for commodity helpdesk, resale-heavy accounts and month-to-month contracts.
caveats: MSP contracts may be cancellable and platforms can capture value through higher software fees; this is not a claim of proprietary technology.

t50_years.rationale: Kaseya reports 53% of MSP respondents already use AI to automate ticketing, patching and monitoring, crossing the frozen 50% boundary for effective operational adoption.
t50_years.plausible_range: 0 now; if the NAICS cohort is below the MSP sample, 1–2 years
caveats: Global MSP survey rather than U.S. 541512 census; it does not establish full autonomous delivery.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Kaseya directly reports that 53% of its more-than-1,000 MSP respondents already use AI for ticketing, patching and monitoring.
caveats: Observed value is for a global MSP sample spanning adjacent IT-service classifications, not a NAICS 541512 sample.

historical_analogs.value: Remote monitoring and management, PSA ticketing and scripted patching shifted MSPs from reactive break-fix hours toward standardized recurring operations; generative AI extends that trajectory into triage, knowledge work and remediation while preserving escalation accountability.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources describe existing ticketing/monitoring automation and recurring pricing structures; the historical analogy is a reasoned interpretation rather than a quantified causal study.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Routine support compresses faster than security ownership and complex projects
caveats: No code-specific historical labor or pricing series was located.

selection.rationale: A deliberately conservative 25% 60-plus estimate: the MSP adviser identifies a broad cohort of 1990s/2000s founders now in their 50s or older, without publishing an exact age distribution.
caveats: Advisory commentary is not a representative 541512 owner-demographic survey.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The source documents an MSP founder-transition problem and cites general small-business evidence that many owners lack a successful buyer or plan; this supports a generic succession gap, not a measured 541512 seller surplus.
caveats: Do not infer the volume, geography or quality of available targets from this qualitative evidence.

active_consolidators.rationale: Conservative count of three distinct active platform/strategic buyer channels evidenced in the report's current MSP transaction examples, including Evergreen's Lyra family, a PE-backed IT solutions acquirer and a sponsor-backed platform.
caveats: Examples are from 1H 2025 and do not enumerate all acquirers or prove interest in every 541512 subsegment.

buy_mult.rationale: Selected near the low end of Aventis's 5–8x smaller-MSP range to reflect a $1–10M EBITDA target with variable customer quality and possible resale revenue.
caveats: The source's disclosed-deal median and size table are not a direct comp set for each screened target.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest scaled-book exit assumption above entry but below broad upper-market benchmarks, contingent on recurring revenue, retention, security/cloud depth and successful integration rather than automatic platform re-rating.
caveats: Aventis's higher transaction data are skewed to larger deals; Solganick reports premium valuations only for differentiated scalable platforms.

pricing_structure.value: Per-user, per-device, combined, tiered-bundle and fixed-fee monthly managed-service billing, plus project and resale revenue
pricing_structure.rationale: Kaseya's benchmark guide lists these pricing models and notes that a per-user agreement changes with a client's employee base.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Recurring managed-service pricing is common; project/resale mix varies materially by target
caveats: The cited pricing mix is from the 2023 benchmark and applies to MSPs, a business-model frame broader than 541512.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. High V reflects routine operating task exposure and A reflects current AI deployment in core MSP workflows. Neither metric assumes the firm can capture savings or that security/accountability demand disappears.

terminal_value.justification: SMB IT, security, backup and recovery still require an accountable managed operator. AI shifts the product from labor-intensive response toward automated, documented and security-led operations; platform disintermediation and seat shrinkage remain material downside cases.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No fallback was used for immutable dataset fields. The role mix is an acknowledged 4-digit 5415 proxy, and every industry source is an MSP/business-model frame that overlaps but does not equal NAICS 541512; both distinctions are retained rather than normalized away.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Subscription billing allows near-term margin capture from automation, but per-user and per-device rates expose the provider to client headcount and endpoint contraction. Commodity ticket reduction can also accelerate price competition or platform-license pass-through at renewal.

dataset_mismatch.description: Frozen employment weights are 4-digit 5415; MSP, systems-integration, cloud, custom-development and outsourced-operations survey populations span 541512 and adjacent 541511, 541513 and 541519 codes.
sensitivity: MSP-heavy targets may have a larger support-desk share than the supplied 15-1232 weight.
Custom-development-heavy targets may have more developer work and less recurring security revenue.
The 39.41% residual can include field technicians and administrative staff with materially different AI exposure.
No frozen values, weights or counts are modified.

subsegments: Managed service providers and systems integrators scored as the dominant investment workflow
Custom software development (541511) excluded except embedded integration work
Client-owned facility/data-center management (541513) and residual IT services (541519) excluded
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: MSP and systems-integration operators with recurring managed-service revenue
market_boundary.selection_basis: Frozen brief plus current MSP adoption, pricing and M&A sources; a target must be screened for recurring-revenue, security and resale/project mix before applying this thesis.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 now; if the 541512 cohort lags the global MSP sample, 1–2 years
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 10 at zero and would fall to 10/t50 if the business-model adoption proxy is not representative.
Subscription pricing supports margin capture, but seats/endpoints, vendor pricing and renewal competition can pass savings through.
M depends on the acquired book becoming a recurring, integrated and differentiated platform asset rather than remaining a small project or resale-heavy operator.

reviewer_note: Fresh live sources were reopened on 2026-07-21. This is a conditional thesis for contract-rich MSP/systems-integration books, not for generic 541512 receipts. The record does not use deal activity to raise automation or adoption, and it does not apply platform multiples to unqualified targets.
